Imine functionalized cross-linked chitosan based bio-sorbent as a pH 
tunable platform for efficient dye adsorption

Vismaya Joseph , Muhammed Arshad , K. Anupriya , Abraham Joseph *

Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Calicut University P O, 673 635, Kerala, India

A R T I C L E I N F O

A B S T R A C T

Keywords:
Chitosan
Imine functionalization
ChDBAG

Chitosan, a biopolymer rich in amino and hydroxyl groups, is a promising material for the development of bio 
sorbents for dye removal from wastewater. In this study, chitosan was functionalized via imine formation with 
3,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde and subsequently crosslinked with glutaraldehyde to obtain a chitosan based 
crosslinked gel (ChDBAG). The material was characterized and compared with unmodified crosslinked chitosan 
gel (ChG) using different techniques, including FTIR, UV–DRS, XRD, TGA, FE-SEM, BET, and XPS. Adsorption 
studies under varying pH conditions demonstrated that ChDBAG exhibits high removal efficiency toward anionic 
dyes (CR, MO, and IC) in acidic media and cationic dyes (MB, MG, and CV) in basic media, with removal effi-
ciencies exceeding 95%. At an optimized adsorbent dosage of 0.2 g/L and a dye concentration of 20 ppm, the 
maximum adsorption capacity for Congo Red was 105.26 mg/g. The adsorption data were analyzed using both 
linear and non-linear fitting of isotherm and kinetic models, revealing the best fit to the Langmuir isotherm and 
pseudo-second-order kinetic model. The mechanism of dye removal is attributed to electrostatic interactions, 
hydrogen bonding, and n–π interactions between the adsorbent and the dyes. Furthermore, desorption studies 
revealed that the adsorbent could be effectively regenerated and reused for up to ten cycles maintaining its high 
removal efficiencies for cationic and anionic dyes. The adsorption capacity was evaluated using dye mixtures 
under variable pH conditions as well as in real water samples. These results highlight the potential of ChDBAG as 
a sustainable and efficient adsorbent for dye-polluted water.

1. Introduction

Human life is profoundly dependent on essential needs such as food, 
water, health, and a sustainable environment. These factors are intri-
cately interconnected, and disruption in any one of them can adversely 
affect the others. Among these necessities, water plays a particularly 
critical role in human life, influencing public health, economic stability, 
environmental sustainability, and resilience to climate change. How-
ever, despite its importance, freshwater resources are extremely limited; 
of the total water available on Earth, only 2.5% constitutes freshwater, 
and merely 0.27% of this fraction is accessible to humans as potable 
water [1–3]. Continued advocacy for equitable access to clean water, 
along with the implementation of sustainable water management 
practices, is essential for ensuring public health and fostering a healthier 
and more prosperous future for all.

Water is an indispensable resource vital for sustaining human life 
and promoting overall health and well-being [4]. Access to clean and 
safe drinking water is crucial for preventing waterborne diseases, which 

remain a significant public health concern in many regions worldwide. 
According to reports by the World Health Organization, nearly 2 billion 
people still lack access to safely managed drinking water, contributing to 
recurrent outbreaks of water-related diseases. Moreover, water scarcity 
and contamination have emerged as critical global challenges, intensi-
fied by rapid population growth, unplanned urbanization, and the 
indiscriminate discharge of industrial effluents. These issues have far- 
reaching consequences for human health, ecosystem stability, and so-
cioeconomic development [1]. As the global population continues to 
expand, projected to reach approximately 9.7 billion by 2050, the de-
mand for freshwater resources is increasing at an unprecedented rate. 
According to the World Resources Institute, by 2040 nearly one in four 
children is expected to live in regions experiencing extremely high- 
water stress. Concurrently, industrial activities contribute substantially 
to water pollution through the direct discharge of untreated or inade-
quately treated effluents containing heavy metals, hazardous chemicals, 
and pathogenic contaminants. Among these industries, the dye and 
textile sector is a major contributor, releasing large volumes of synthetic

dyes and toxic substances into aquatic environments. The United Na-
tions Environment Programme (UNEP) reports that nearly 80% of 
wastewater generated globally is discharged into the environment 
without adequate treatment. Such uncontrolled pollution not only dis-
rupts aquatic ecosystems but also poses serious health risks to commu-
nities' dependent on contaminated water sources. Addressing this 
escalating environmental challenge requires the enforcement of strin-
gent regulatory frameworks governing industrial effluent discharge [5], 
alongside the adoption of advanced wastewater treatment technologies 
and comprehensive water quality monitoring strategies to protect 
environmental sustainability and public health.

Wastewater management aims to emphasize the removal of synthetic 
dyes through a more efficacious methodology [6]. Presently, numerous 
techniques such as photocatalysis [7–10], electrochemical oxidation 
[11,12], coagulation or flocculation [13], and ion exchange [14,15] are 

utilized [16]. However, these approaches are encumbered by significant 
drawbacks, including excessive energy and chemical consumption, 
intricate operational procedures, prohibitive costs, and the production 
of hazardous by-products [17,18]. As a result, we have selected an 
efficient, operationally viable, and economically advantageous adsorp-
tion technique as the most optimal and pragmatic solution for dye 
abatement [19–21]. To address this, we have developed adsorbent sys-
tem based on chitosan, the second most abundant polymer in nature, 
derived from marine organisms [22–24]. The development of chitosan 
as an adsorbent is limited by inherent drawbacks; therefore, various 
modification strategies such as crosslinking [22,25–29], incorporation 
of metal oxide nanoparticles [16,30], and grafting with multiwalled 
carbon nanotubes [31–35] are employed to enhance its performance. In 
this study the chitosan linear biopolymer was chemically altered

dihydroxybenzaldehyde and glutaraldehyde, respectively, to augment 
its physicochemical properties and enhance its porosity-driven adsorp-
tive capabilities. These modifications were meticulously designed to 
mitigate the inherent drawbacks of unmodified chitosan, such as its 
elevated solubility in acidic conditions, leachability, pronounced 
swelling behavior, susceptibility to degradation, and propensity to form 
colloidal aggregates in aqueous environments [16,36–40]. Additionally, 
the imine bond formed introduces more coordination sites, enhancing 
the chemical robustness of the polymer matrix. The aldehyde chosen for 
imine functionalization, 3,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde, possesses multiple 

hydroxyl groups on the aromatic ring, which provide numerous 
adsorption-active functionalities and can act as hydrogen bond donors 
and acceptors, facilitating strong interactions with both cationic and 
anionic dyes. Moreover, it imparts increased surface polarity and pH- 
responsive behavior, enabling selective and tunable electrostatic in-
teractions. The modifications applied to the chitosan Schiff base-based 
hydrogel derivative confer a highly efficient, tunable, pH-dependent 
dye removal capacity by enhancing its chemical interactions with both 
cationic and anionic dyes [41–45]. This biosorbent exhibits removal 
efficiencies exceeding 95% for anionic dyes such as Congo Red (CR), 

3

Methyl Orange (MO), and Indigo Carmine (IC) under neutral pH con-
ditions, owing to its naturally cationic charge in the gel state. Further-
more, it can be finely tuned to exhibit a negative charge in alkaline 
environments, enabling the effective removal of cationic dyes such as 
Methylene Blue (MB), Crystal Violet (CV), and Malachite Green (MG). 
The imine functionalization with a hydroxyl-rich moiety imparts selec-
tivity toward both anionic and cationic dyes by modulating surface in-
teractions under different pH conditions. To the best of our knowledge, 
this is the first study to develop a dual-charge pH tunable chitosan Schiff 
base hydrogel capable of efficiently removing both anionic and cationic 
dyes. The work demonstrates the enhanced adsorption performance of 
the hydrogel under variable pH conditions, its reusability over multiple 
cycles, and its applicability in real water samples. These findings un-
derscore the novelty and significance of this biosorbent, providing a 
versatile, sustainable, and highly efficient material for the remediation 
of dye-polluted water.

2. Experimental section

2.1. Chemicals and materials

Chemicals of superior purity, namely chitosan, 3, 4-dihydroxyben-
zaldehyde, and glutaraldehyde, were procured from Sigma-Aldrich 
and TCI, and used without further purification. The solvents and dyes 
employed, including Congo Red (CR), Methyl Orange (MO), Indigo 
Carmine (IC), Methylene Blue (MB), Malachite Green (MG), and Crystal 
Violet (CV), were of analytical grade. Double-distilled water was 
employed throughout the investigation.

2.2. Synthesis of adsorbents

2.2.1. Synthesis of chitosan crosslinked gel (ChG)

The preparation of the chitosan unmodified gel (ChG) was executed 
as previously described [29], with certain modifications (Scheme 1). 
Approximately 1 g of chitosan was weighed and dissolved in 50 mL of a 
2% acetic acid solution, and stirred overnight. The resulting solution 
was then filtered through cheesecloth to remove any undissolved chi-
tosan particles. Then, 10 mL of ethanol solution was added and mixed 
thoroughly to achieve a uniform composition. Subsequently, 5 mL of a 
◦
10% glutaraldehyde solution was incorporated at 70 
C, and the mixture 
was stirred continuously for 3 h. The reaction mixture was then 
precipitated with a 5% NaOH solution and rinsed with ethanol to 
remove any residual unreacted aldehyde. The precipitate was washed 
multiple times with distilled water until the pH reached 7. The final 
product was dried and ground into a fine powder for characterization 
and further studies.

2.2.2. Synthesis of chitosan-Schiff base crosslinked gel (ChDBAG)

The synthesis of the chitosan-based Schiff base gel (ChDBAG) was 
conducted as previously reported [29], with some modifications 
(Scheme 2). Approximately 1 g of chitosan was weighed and dissolved in 
50 mL of a 2% acetic acid solution, with stirring overnight. The resulting 
solution was filtered through cheesecloth to eliminate undissolved chi-
tosan. Then, 10 mL of ethanol solution was added and stirred to achieve 
a homogeneous mixture. Subsequently, 2.1408 g of 3,4-dihydroxyben-
zaldehyde was introduced into the ethanol solution, maintained at 
◦
70 
C, followed by stirring for 6 h. Then, 5 mL of a 10% glutaraldehyde 
solution was added at the same temperature, and the mixture was stirred 
for another 1 h. The reaction mixture was precipitated with 5% NaOH 
solution and washed with ethanol to remove any unreacted aldehyde. 
Afterward, the precipitate was washed multiple times with distilled 
water until the pH reached 7. The final product was dried and powdered 
for subsequent characterization. The characterizations were compared 
with the chitosan gel (Ch-DBAG) that did not contain 3, 4- 
dihydroxybenzaldehyde.

2.4. Adsorption batch experiments

Adsorption batch experiments were systematically conducted to 
evaluate the sequestration efficacy of the synthesized adsorbent toward 
both cationic dyes (Methylene Blue (MB), Malachite Green (MG), Crystal 
Violet (CV)) and anionic dyes (Congo Red (CR), Methyl Orange (MO), 
Indigo Carmine (IC)). A stock solution of 1000 ppm was prepared for 
each dye and used in all adsorption trials. For single-dye experiments, 
the adsorbent dosage and initial dye concentration were optimized using 
CR in a neutral medium as a reference, allowing coherent comparison 
across all dye systems. For the optimization of adsorbent dosage and 
initial dye concentration, 50 mL solutions of Congo Red (CR) in water, at 
different concentrations (2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, and 20 ppm) were prepared. 
These solutions were treated with varying amounts of the adsorbent 
(0.06, 0.1, 0.14, 0.2, 0.6, and 1 g/L) under controlled conditions. Each 
adsorption trial was allowed to reach equilibrium through systematic 
incubation, after which the supernatants were separated by centrifuga-
tion. The residual dye concentrations were quantified using UV–Visible 
spectrophotometry, and the removal efficiency and adsorption capacity 
for each trial were calculated using standard equations.

presented in the Fig. 3. The observed color change in the adsorbent 
further confirms the successful adsorption of the dye molecules. The 
Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis exhibited a pronounced in-
crease in the average pore diameter of the modified adsorbent ChDBAG 
(236 nm) relative to ChG (104 nm). This enlargement indicates that the 

chemical modification effectively reconfigured the chitosan matrix, 
generating additional voids and reducing its structural compactness. The 
resulting porous framework facilitates improved surface accessibility 
and accelerated diffusion of dye molecules, thereby enhancing the 
overall adsorption efficiency. The pore diameter range indicates a 
macroporous nature, characterized by expansive, open channels that 
promote enhanced molecular diffusion, optimize accessibility to active 
sites, and enable facile desorption.

2. Experimental section

2.1. Chemicals and materials

Chemicals of superior purity, namely chitosan, 3, 4-dihydroxyben-
zaldehyde, and glutaraldehyde, were procured from Sigma-Aldrich 
and TCI, and used without further purification. The solvents and dyes 
employed, including Congo Red (CR), Methyl Orange (MO), Indigo 
Carmine (IC), Methylene Blue (MB), Malachite Green (MG), and Crystal 
Violet (CV), were of analytical grade. Double-distilled water was 
employed throughout the investigation.

2.2. Synthesis of adsorbents

2.2.1. Synthesis of chitosan crosslinked gel (ChG)

The preparation of the chitosan unmodified gel (ChG) was executed 
as previously described [29], with certain modifications (Scheme 1). 
Approximately 1 g of chitosan was weighed and dissolved in 50 mL of a 
2% acetic acid solution, and stirred overnight. The resulting solution 
was then filtered through cheesecloth to remove any undissolved chi-
tosan particles. Then, 10 mL of ethanol solution was added and mixed 
thoroughly to achieve a uniform composition. Subsequently, 5 mL of a 
◦
10% glutaraldehyde solution was incorporated at 70 
C, and the mixture 
was stirred continuously for 3 h. The reaction mixture was then 
precipitated with a 5% NaOH solution and rinsed with ethanol to 
remove any residual unreacted aldehyde. The precipitate was washed 
multiple times with distilled water until the pH reached 7. The final 
product was dried and ground into a fine powder for characterization 
and further studies.

2.2.2. Synthesis of chitosan-Schiff base crosslinked gel (ChDBAG)

The synthesis of the chitosan-based Schiff base gel (ChDBAG) was 
conducted as previously reported [29], with some modifications 
(Scheme 2). Approximately 1 g of chitosan was weighed and dissolved in 
50 mL of a 2% acetic acid solution, with stirring overnight. The resulting 
solution was filtered through cheesecloth to eliminate undissolved chi-
tosan. Then, 10 mL of ethanol solution was added and stirred to achieve 
a homogeneous mixture. Subsequently, 2.1408 g of 3,4-dihydroxyben-
zaldehyde was introduced into the ethanol solution, maintained at 
◦
70 
C, followed by stirring for 6 h. Then, 5 mL of a 10% glutaraldehyde 
solution was added at the same temperature, and the mixture was stirred 
for another 1 h. The reaction mixture was precipitated with 5% NaOH 
solution and washed with ethanol to remove any unreacted aldehyde. 
Afterward, the precipitate was washed multiple times with distilled 
water until the pH reached 7. The final product was dried and powdered 
for subsequent characterization. The characterizations were compared 
with the chitosan gel (Ch-DBAG) that did not contain 3, 4- 
dihydroxybenzaldehyde.

2.4. Adsorption batch experiments

Adsorption batch experiments were systematically conducted to 
evaluate the sequestration efficacy of the synthesized adsorbent toward 
both cationic dyes (Methylene Blue (MB), Malachite Green (MG), Crystal 
Violet (CV)) and anionic dyes (Congo Red (CR), Methyl Orange (MO), 
Indigo Carmine (IC)). A stock solution of 1000 ppm was prepared for 
each dye and used in all adsorption trials. For single-dye experiments, 
the adsorbent dosage and initial dye concentration were optimized using 
CR in a neutral medium as a reference, allowing coherent comparison 
across all dye systems. For the optimization of adsorbent dosage and 
initial dye concentration, 50 mL solutions of Congo Red (CR) in water, at 
different concentrations (2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, and 20 ppm) were prepared. 
These solutions were treated with varying amounts of the adsorbent 
(0.06, 0.1, 0.14, 0.2, 0.6, and 1 g/L) under controlled conditions. Each 
adsorption trial was allowed to reach equilibrium through systematic 
incubation, after which the supernatants were separated by centrifuga-
tion. The residual dye concentrations were quantified using UV–Visible 
spectrophotometry, and the removal efficiency and adsorption capacity 
for each trial were calculated using standard equations.

Imine functionalized cross-linked chitosan based bio-sorbent as a pH 
tunable platform for efficient dye adsorption

Vismaya Joseph , Muhammed Arshad , K. Anupriya , Abraham Joseph *

Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Calicut University P O, 673 635, Kerala, India

A R T I C L E I N F O

A B S T R A C T

Keywords:
Chitosan
Imine functionalization
ChDBAG

Chitosan, a biopolymer rich in amino and hydroxyl groups, is a promising material for the development of bio 
sorbents for dye removal from wastewater. In this study, chitosan was functionalized via imine formation with 
3,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde and subsequently crosslinked with glutaraldehyde to obtain a chitosan based 
crosslinked gel (ChDBAG). The material was characterized and compared with unmodified crosslinked chitosan 
gel (ChG) using different techniques, including FTIR, UV–DRS, XRD, TGA, FE-SEM, BET, and XPS. Adsorption 
studies under varying pH conditions demonstrated that ChDBAG exhibits high removal efficiency toward anionic 
dyes (CR, MO, and IC) in acidic media and cationic dyes (MB, MG, and CV) in basic media, with removal effi-
ciencies exceeding 95%. At an optimized adsorbent dosage of 0.2 g/L and a dye concentration of 20 ppm, the 
maximum adsorption capacity for Congo Red was 105.26 mg/g. The adsorption data were analyzed using both 
linear and non-linear fitting of isotherm and kinetic models, revealing the best fit to the Langmuir isotherm and 
pseudo-second-order kinetic model. The mechanism of dye removal is attributed to electrostatic interactions, 
hydrogen bonding, and n–π interactions between the adsorbent and the dyes. Furthermore, desorption studies 
revealed that the adsorbent could be effectively regenerated and reused for up to ten cycles maintaining its high 
removal efficiencies for cationic and anionic dyes. The adsorption capacity was evaluated using dye mixtures 
under variable pH conditions as well as in real water samples. These results highlight the potential of ChDBAG as 
a sustainable and efficient adsorbent for dye-polluted water.

1. Introduction

Human life is profoundly dependent on essential needs such as food, 
water, health, and a sustainable environment. These factors are intri-
cately interconnected, and disruption in any one of them can adversely 
affect the others. Among these necessities, water plays a particularly 
critical role in human life, influencing public health, economic stability, 
environmental sustainability, and resilience to climate change. How-
ever, despite its importance, freshwater resources are extremely limited; 
of the total water available on Earth, only 2.5% constitutes freshwater, 
and merely 0.27% of this fraction is accessible to humans as potable 
water [1–3]. Continued advocacy for equitable access to clean water, 
along with the implementation of sustainable water management 
practices, is essential for ensuring public health and fostering a healthier 
and more prosperous future for all.

Water is an indispensable resource vital for sustaining human life 
and promoting overall health and well-being [4]. Access to clean and 
safe drinking water is crucial for preventing waterborne diseases, which 

remain a significant public health concern in many regions worldwide. 
According to reports by the World Health Organization, nearly 2 billion 
people still lack access to safely managed drinking water, contributing to 
recurrent outbreaks of water-related diseases. Moreover, water scarcity 
and contamination have emerged as critical global challenges, intensi-
fied by rapid population growth, unplanned urbanization, and the 
indiscriminate discharge of industrial effluents. These issues have far- 
reaching consequences for human health, ecosystem stability, and so-
cioeconomic development [1]. As the global population continues to 
expand, projected to reach approximately 9.7 billion by 2050, the de-
mand for freshwater resources is increasing at an unprecedented rate. 
According to the World Resources Institute, by 2040 nearly one in four 
children is expected to live in regions experiencing extremely high- 
water stress. Concurrently, industrial activities contribute substantially 
to water pollution through the direct discharge of untreated or inade-
quately treated effluents containing heavy metals, hazardous chemicals, 
and pathogenic contaminants. Among these industries, the dye and 
textile sector is a major contributor, releasing large volumes of synthetic

2.2. Synthesis of adsorbents

2.2.1. Synthesis of chitosan crosslinked gel (ChG)

The preparation of the chitosan unmodified gel (ChG) was executed 
as previously described [29], with certain modifications (Scheme 1). 
Approximately 1 g of chitosan was weighed and dissolved in 50 mL of a 
2% acetic acid solution, and stirred overnight. The resulting solution 
was then filtered through cheesecloth to remove any undissolved chi-
tosan particles. Then, 10 mL of ethanol solution was added and mixed 
thoroughly to achieve a uniform composition. Subsequently, 5 mL of a 
◦
10% glutaraldehyde solution was incorporated at 70 
C, and the mixture 
was stirred continuously for 3 h. The reaction mixture was then 
precipitated with a 5% NaOH solution and rinsed with ethanol to 
remove any residual unreacted aldehyde. The precipitate was washed 
multiple times with distilled water until the pH reached 7. The final 
product was dried and ground into a fine powder for characterization 
and further studies.

2.2.2. Synthesis of chitosan-Schiff base crosslinked gel (ChDBAG)

The synthesis of the chitosan-based Schiff base gel (ChDBAG) was 
conducted as previously reported [29], with some modifications 
(Scheme 2). Approximately 1 g of chitosan was weighed and dissolved in 
50 mL of a 2% acetic acid solution, with stirring overnight. The resulting 
solution was filtered through cheesecloth to eliminate undissolved chi-
tosan. Then, 10 mL of ethanol solution was added and stirred to achieve 
a homogeneous mixture. Subsequently, 2.1408 g of 3,4-dihydroxyben-
zaldehyde was introduced into the ethanol solution, maintained at 
◦
70 
C, followed by stirring for 6 h. Then, 5 mL of a 10% glutaraldehyde 
solution was added at the same temperature, and the mixture was stirred 
for another 1 h. The reaction mixture was precipitated with 5% NaOH 
solution and washed with ethanol to remove any unreacted aldehyde. 
Afterward, the precipitate was washed multiple times with distilled 
water until the pH reached 7. The final product was dried and powdered 
for subsequent characterization. The characterizations were compared 
with the chitosan gel (Ch-DBAG) that did not contain 3, 4- 
dihydroxybenzaldehyde.

2.4. Adsorption batch experiments

Adsorption batch experiments were systematically conducted to 
evaluate the sequestration efficacy of the synthesized adsorbent toward 
both cationic dyes (Methylene Blue (MB), Malachite Green (MG), Crystal 
Violet (CV)) and anionic dyes (Congo Red (CR), Methyl Orange (MO), 
Indigo Carmine (IC)). A stock solution of 1000 ppm was prepared for 
each dye and used in all adsorption trials. For single-dye experiments, 
the adsorbent dosage and initial dye concentration were optimized using 
CR in a neutral medium as a reference, allowing coherent comparison 
across all dye systems. For the optimization of adsorbent dosage and 
initial dye concentration, 50 mL solutions of Congo Red (CR) in water, at 
different concentrations (2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, and 20 ppm) were prepared. 
These solutions were treated with varying amounts of the adsorbent 
(0.06, 0.1, 0.14, 0.2, 0.6, and 1 g/L) under controlled conditions. Each 
adsorption trial was allowed to reach equilibrium through systematic 
incubation, after which the supernatants were separated by centrifuga-
tion. The residual dye concentrations were quantified using UV–Visible 
spectrophotometry, and the removal efficiency and adsorption capacity 
for each trial were calculated using standard equations.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the synthesized ad-

the third stage exhibited slower degradation and increased char residue 
formation. The surface morphology of the samples was analyzed using

presented in the Fig. 3. The observed color change in the adsorbent 
further confirms the successful adsorption of the dye molecules. The 
Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis exhibited a pronounced in-
crease in the average pore diameter of the modified adsorbent ChDBAG 
(236 nm) relative to ChG (104 nm). This enlargement indicates that the 

chemical modification effectively reconfigured the chitosan matrix, 
generating additional voids and reducing its structural compactness. The 
resulting porous framework facilitates improved surface accessibility 
and accelerated diffusion of dye molecules, thereby enhancing the 
overall adsorption efficiency. The pore diameter range indicates a 
macroporous nature, characterized by expansive, open channels that 
promote enhanced molecular diffusion, optimize accessibility to active 
sites, and enable facile desorption.

chemical modification of the chitosan backbone. Although imine (C=N)
